coredata.dc:title: Curauá-derived carbon dots: Fluorescent probes for effective Fe(III) ion detection, cellular labeling and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Raja, Sebastian
Buhl, Eva Miriam
Dreschers, Stephan
Schalla, Carmen
Zenke, Martin
Sechi, Antonio
Mattoso, Luiz H.C.
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the generation of curauá-derived carbon dots (C-dots) and their suitability for Fe(III) detection, bioimaging and FACS analysis. C-dots were generated from curauá (Ananas erectifolius) fibers by a facile one-step hydrothermal approach. They exhibited graphite-like structure with a mean diameter of 2.4 nm, high water solubility, high levels of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, excitation-dependent multicolor fluorescence emission (in the range 450 nm - 560 nm) and superior photostability. C-dots were highly selective and effective for the detection of ferric Fe(III) ion in an aqueous medium with a detection limit of 0.77 μM in the linear range of 0–30 μM, a value much lower than the guideline limits proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO). In biological cell systems, C-dots were very well tolerated by B16F1 mouse melanoma and J774.A1 mouse macrophages cell lines, both of which effectively internalized C-dots in their cytoplasmic compartment. Finally, C-dots were effective probes for long-term live cell imaging experiments and multi-channel flow cytometry analysis. Collectively, our findings demonstrate that curauá-derived C-dots serve as versatile and effective natural products for Fe(III) ion sensing, labeling and bioimaging of various cell types. This study adds novel C-dots to the library of carbon-based probes and paves the way towards a sustainable conversion of a most abundant biomass waste into value-added products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curauá
Carbon dots
Fe(III) ion sensing
Fluorescent probe
Photostability
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of intracellular C-dots
Flow cytometry analysis of intracellular C-dots
C-dots derived from curauá fibers are highly cytocompatible
Cellular internalization and localization of C-dots
Detection and visualization of C-dots by fluorescence microscopy
Curauá-derived C-dots are suitable for long-term live cell imaging
Flow cytometry analysis of intracellular C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo
Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Inovação

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank FAPESP (Proc. No. 2015/00094-0; Proc. No. 2017/22017-3), MCTI/SISNANO, REDEAGRONANO for financial support. They also thank Chaolei Hu and Andrij Pich (DWI-Leibniz-Institute for Interactive Materials, RWTH Aachen University) for their assistance in XPS measurement, S. Anbu (Departments of Chemistry and Biomedical Sciences, University of Hull, UK) for his assistance in ion sensing analysis and Shabir Najmudin (Randall Centre, King's College, London, UK) for the revision of English language.

The authors thank FAPESP (Proc. No. 2015/00094-0 ; Proc. No. 2017/22017-3 ), MCTI/SISNANO , REDEAGRONANO for financial support. They also thank Chaolei Hu and Andrij Pich (DWI-Leibniz-Institute for Interactive Materials, RWTH Aachen University) for their assistance in XPS measurement, S. Anbu (Departments of Chemistry and Biomedical Sciences, University of Hull, UK) for his assistance in ion sensing analysis and Shabir Najmudin (Randall Centre, King's College, London, UK) for the revision of English language.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterisations of chemical functionality, morphology, and size of C-dots. (A) Representative ATR-FTIR spectrum showing the main C-dots functional groups. (B) Size distribution of C-dots obtained from the analysis of several TEM images. (C) Representative TEM image of C-dots spotted on a carbon support. Scale bar: 25 nm. (D) AFM image of C-dots. The white line indicates the region scanned to obtain the height profile shown in (E). Legend on the right side of (D) indicates the height of the C-dots. (F) 3D representation of a characteristic AFM profile of C-dots. (G-I) XPS analysis of C-dots showing a survey spectrum (G) and high-resolution spectra of O1s (H) and C 1 s (I).

Physico-chemical characterizations of C-dots. (A) UV–visible, excitation and emission profile of C-dots. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 520 nm. (C) Representative time-course analysis of the photostability of C-dots and the commercial due Rhodamine B (RhB) under continuous irradiation with light at 480 nm.

PL intensity profile of C-dots exposed to Fe(III) ions in aqueous solutions under excitation with light at 340 nm. (A) PL intensity (fluorescence quenching) profile of C-dots (50 μg/mL) incubated with 100 μM FeCl3. (B) Gradual decrease of PL intensity induced by titrating an aqueous C-dots solution with increasing amount of FeCl3 (0–135 μM). (C) Analysis of relationship between the PL intensity ratio (F0/F) and the concentration of Fe(III) ions in the range between 0 and 135 μM. (D) Linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio (F0/F) and the concentration of Fe (III) in the range between 0 and 30 μM.

Impact of C-dots on the viability of B16F1 and J774.A1 cells. (A) The viability of B16F1 incubated with various concentrations of C-dots for 24 h at 37 °C is mostly not affected averaging approximately 96%. By contrast, after 48 h of incubation with C-dots, their viability decreases to approximately 50–60%. (B) The viability of J77A.A1 incubated with the same concentrations of C-dots for 24 h at 37 °C is reduced to 70–80%. After 48 h of incubation with C-dots, the viability of J774.A1 is strongly decreased averaging 40%. Cell viability is expressed as percentage of untreated cells set to 100%. Histograms show mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). p < 0.0001 (**).

Transmission electron microscopy analysis of B16F1 and J774.A1 cells incubated with C-dots. (A) TEM image of free C-dots (50 μg/mL). In B16F1 (B) and J774.A1 (C) cells with C-dots (50 μg/mL) for 24 h at 37 °C, C-dots localize in the cytoplasm (see red box in B) and in vacuoles (see red box in C). Arrows in the insets point to the typical spherical structure of C-dots (compare with A). Scale bars: 25 nm (for A, B and C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence microscopy analysis of intracellular C-dots. After incubation with C-dots (100 μg/mL), B16F1 and J774.A1 cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, washed with PBS and then mounted on glass slides. In both cell types, C-dots can be visualized after excitation using both Alexa 488 and Alexa 594 filter sets. Arrows point to groups of C-dots in the cytoplasm of both cell types. Dashed red ellipses in A and D show the position and contours of the nuclei. Scale bars: 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Time-lapse imaging of C-dots in B16F1 and J774.A1cells. Cells were incubated with C-dots (100 μg/mL) for 24 h at 37 °C, washed with PBS and then filmed every 5 min for 24 h by dual channel microscopy. In both cell types, C-dots can be clearly continuously imaged for 24 h with a negligible loss of their fluorescence signal. Numbers in A and B indicate the elapsed time in hours. Scale bars: 50 μm.

Flowcytometry analysis of B16F1 and J774.A1 incubated with C-dots. Histograms showing the uptake of C-dots uptake by B16F1 cells (A-E) and J774.A1 (F-J) at 1, 24, 48 and 72 h monitored using different fluorescence channels. Note the increase of fluorescence signal at later time points (24, 48 and 72 h), particularly in the FITC and PE channels. All histograms are representative of 4 measurements from 2 independent experiments.

Quantification of C-dots internalization in B16F1 and J774.A1 by flow cytometry analyses. Bar charts A-D refer to B16F1 cells, whereas bar charts E-H refer to J77.A1 macrophages. All bars show the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI, ± SD; n = 3) calculated for each fluorescence channel. B16F1 and J774.A1 cells were incubated with C-dots (100 μg/mL) for 1, 24, 48 or 72 h. Numbers indicate p values; ns: non-significant.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of curauá C-dots.
Detection of Fe(III) ions by representative C-dots derived from various natural sources.

head.ce:title: Curauá-derived carbon dots: Fluorescent probes for effective Fe(III) ion detection, cellular labeling and bioimaging

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Nanotechnology Laboratory for Agribusiness (LNNA), Embrapa Instrumentação, São Carlos, SP 13560-970, Brazil.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the generation of curauá-derived carbon dots (C-dots) and their suitability for Fe(III) detection, bioimaging and FACS analysis. C-dots were generated from curauá (Ananas erectifolius) fibers by a facile one-step hydrothermal approach. They exhibited graphite-like structure with a mean diameter of 2.4 nm, high water solubility, high levels of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, excitation-dependent multicolor fluorescence emission (in the range 450 nm - 560 nm) and superior photostability. C-dots were highly selective and effective for the detection of ferric Fe(III) ion in an aqueous medium with a detection limit of 0.77 μM in the linear range of 0–30 μM, a value much lower than the guideline limits proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO). In biological cell systems, C-dots were very well tolerated by B16F1 mouse melanoma and J774.A1 mouse macrophages cell lines, both of which effectively internalized C-dots in their cytoplasmic compartment. Finally, C-dots were effective probes for long-term live cell imaging experiments and multi-channel flow cytometry analysis. Collectively, our findings demonstrate that curauá-derived C-dots serve as versatile and effective natural products for Fe(III) ion sensing, labeling and bioimaging of various cell types. This study adds novel C-dots to the library of carbon-based probes and paves the way towards a sustainable conversion of a most abundant biomass waste into value-added products.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass, a major renewable resource of carbon on earth, derives from agricultural residues, forest by-products, industrial residuals, and food wastes. Conversion of such low-cost biomass waste into value-added product is highly desirable for environmental-friendly disposal and the production of high-performance materials aimed at addressing various issues in modern society [1,2]. The development of nanotechnology has led to many efforts being directed towards novel biomass materials including carbon dots (C-dots), carbon nanotubes and bi-composite materials for numerous applications in energy, environmental and biological fields [3–11].

C-dots [12] are a novel class of emerging nanoparticles (NPs) mostly composed of carbon atoms exhibiting a quasi-spherical morphology and sizes typically smaller than 10 nm [13–15]. C-dots can be easily synthesized following either a “top-down” or a “bottom-up” approach by using various synthetic [16,17] and natural precursors [18–20]. The natural precursors, due to their low-cost, renewable carbon source, wide availability, extremely low environmental impact and an easy synthetic approach are preferable over synthetic precursors and have attracted much attention for the production of C-dots. Several natural resources including sweet potato [21], curcumin [22], green pepper seeds [23], spinach [24], food wastes [25], lychee waste [26,27], orange peel [28], rose petals [29], kidney beans [30], sugar [31], sugarcane molasses [32], lignin [33], cellulose [34–36], and bio-refinery byproducts [37] have been explored for developing C-dots via various synthetic routes such as laser ablation [38], arc discharge [39], electrochemical oxidation [40], hydrothermal [41], microwave [42] and pyrolysis [43]. Their unique properties including strong photoluminescence (PL), high photostability, robust chemical inertness and easy chemical modification make C-dots promising candidates for ion sensing, catalysis, photovoltaic, optoelectronics, nanomedicine and bioimaging applications [44–47]. In the context of ion sensing, C-dots are particularly suitable for the detection of Fe(III) ions, which are very important for the regulation of several biological processes including oxygen-carrying, intracellular ion transport, enzymatic reactions and various biosynthetic pathways [48,49]. Plasma-optical emission spectrometry [50], electrochemical [51], colorimetry [51] and atomic absorption spectrometry [52] have been used for C-dot-based detection of Fe(III), although several studies used fluorescence spectrometry due to its simplicity, good selectivity, high sensitivity and rapid detection of metal (Mn+) ions [53]. Indeed, C-dots-based fluorescence detection of Fe(III) ions in aqueous systems has gained growing attention and provided a limit-of-detection (LOD) for Fe(III) ions as low as 0.03 μM in the range of 0.05–500 μM [30,44,54–64]. Hence, given that traditional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (SQDS), organic dyes and polymer dots exhibit toxicity, serious health and environmental issues [65], the very low or non-toxic nature of C-dots makes them a potential and competitive alternative to the above-mentioned fluorescent materials.

The generation of C-dots from natural resources in a larger scale with improved physicochemical properties remains a challenging task and their generation from forest byproducts, in particular those based on cellulose, is currently limited [19,34–36]. Curauá (Ananas erectifolius) [66] is a significant biomass precursor that is considered as one of the most abundant palm species in Brazil. Lignocellulosic fibers of curauá have a great potential in industrial settings due to their high content of cellulose (70.2%), hemicellulose (18.3%) and lignin (9.3%). These components serve as a source of carbon (C), hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O) elements for the formation of C-dots with hydroxyl (OH) and carboxyl (COOH) surface functionalities, which facilitates high aqueous solubility and fluorescence property for potential applications in ion sensing and bioimaging. To the best of our knowledge, the exploration of curauá as the green resource for the synthesis of C-dots has not been reported to date, despite being used as reinforcement in polymer matrices [67].

In this study, we describe the preparation of highly fluorescent C-dots by a facile, green and low-cost hydrothermal synthetic approach using curauá fibers as the precursor. C-dots exhibited an almost spherical morphology with a mean size of 2.4 nm. They also exhibited high water solubility C-dots, robust levels of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on their surface, strong excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, superior photostability and effective Fe(III) ion detection. In addition, in biological cell systems, we demonstrate that curauá-derived C-dots can be used as very effective probes for biolabeling, long-term live cell imaging and flow cytometry analysis.

Curauá fibers were donated by Embrapa Pantanal, Brazil. The stock solution of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) were prepared from Cr2O3 and K2Cr2O7, respectively. The stock solutions of metal ions were prepared from their chloride salts. All the reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. A Cell-Quant™MTT Cell Proliferation Assay Kit (Catalog Number: A015) was supplied from GeneCopoeia™. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The C-dots were prepared using a simple hydrothermal approach from curauá fibers. Briefly, the mixture of curauá fibers (1.00 g) and 1 M sodium hydroxide (20 mL; 1 M NaOH) was taken into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL). Then, the mixture was heated up to180 °C for 12 h. Afterward, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was filtered and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove black insoluble residues. The brownish-yellow supernatant was dialyzed against MilliQ water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000 Da) for 7 days (water was replaced every 24 h). Finally, the dialyzed solution was collected and freeze-dried in a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain C-dots as brownish-yellow solid with 31% of yield.

Infrared spectra of dried C-dots were analyzed by the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) method using a spectrophotometer (Vertex 70, Bruker, Germany). For all samples, spectra were collected at a resolution of 2 cm−1 in the range between 3750 and 500 cm−1 for a total of 128 scans. For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses of C-dots, C-dots in solution were allowed to adsorb onto glow discharged formvar‑carbon-coated nickel grids (Maxtaform, 200 mesh, Plano, Wetzlar, Germany) for 10 min. Subsequently, liquid was removed with filter paper. The grid was stained with a 0.5% solution of uranyl acetate, 1% lead citrate (both from EMS, Germany) and dried at room temperature. TEM images were recorded with a Leo 906 transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany) using a backscattered detector. The apparent zeta (ζ) potential of C-dots (at 0.01 wt% dispersions in water) was determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano particle analyzer. UV–Vis spectroscopy was done using a Double Beam spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV 6300PC equipment). Photoluminescent (PL) emission spectral studies were performed using a RF-5301PC Fluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan). XPS measurements were carried out in an Ultra AxisTM spectrometer (Kratos Analytical, Manchester UK). For this purpose, C-dots were irradiated with monoenergetic Al Kα1,2 radiation (1486.6 eV) and the spectra were taken at a power of 144 W (12 kV × 12 mA). Quantification was done using the CasaXPS software (http://www.casaxps.com/). AFM measurements were done in tapping mode using a Nanosurf atomic force microscope (Model: Flex). For AFM, C-dots were spotted on the surface of mica and dried in a desiccator before analysis. Quantifications were carried out using the Gwyddion software (http://gwyddion.net/).

Routinely, C-dots (25 mg) were dissolved in 500 mL of MilliQ water to make a stock solution (50 μg/mL). 4 mL of stock solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette and mixed with 40 μL of 10 mM metal ions solution and allowed to interact for 2 min at RT. Afterward, fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm at room temperature. Under these experimental condition, selectivity of C-dots for Fe(III) ion was demonstrated in the presence of potential interfering ions at a molar ratio of 1: 2. To test the sensing efficiency of C-dots for Fe(III), various concentrations (0–500 μM) of Fe(III) were prepared from stock solutions (1 mM) and titrated using C-dots (50 μg/mL). The gradual quenching of C-dots fluorescence induced by increasing concentrations of Fe(III) ions was measured by PL spectroscopy.

To determine the impact of pH on the PL emission of C-dots, PL spectra of aqueous C-dot solutions (50 μg/mL) at various pH (range between 2 and 12) were acquired using a RF-5301PC Fluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan) upon excitation at 340 nm at room temperature.

The absorption or PL emission intensity of C-dots with various irradiation time under UV light (Deuterium (D2) lamp; L6380) was tested. In a typical procedure, 10 mL aqueous solution of C-dots (0.5 mg) or Rhodamine B (RhB) (0.4 mg) solution was irradiated by UV light in a closed hood at a regular interval for 1 h, and then, the corresponding PL spectrum was acquired in RF-5301PC Fluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan). PL intensity of C-dots and RhB against various time points were calculated and compared.

B16F1 mouse melanoma cells and J774.A1 mouse macrophages were grown at 37 °C, 5% CO2 in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) high glucose supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum (FCS), 2 mM l-glutamine, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 100 μg/mL streptomycin, and 100 U/mL penicillin as already described [68,69].

The viability of B16F1 and J774.A1 cells exposed to C-dots was measured by MTT (3-[4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazol]-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide) assay. Briefly, cells were plated at 5 × 103 cells/well in a 96-well plate. After 24 h of incubation at 37 °C in a humid, 5% CO2 and 95% air atmosphere, the culture medium was discarded and replaced with 100 μL of fresh DMEM medium containing C-dots at a concentration of 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 or 300 μg/mL. Cells without C-dots served as controls. After 24 or 48 h of additional incubation, 10 μL of PBS containing MTT (5 mg/mL) reagent was added to each well. Cells were incubated for another 4 h, then the medium was discarded and the formazan product (solubilized with 100 μL of SDS in 0.01 M HCl) was added to the cells. After overnight incubation in the dark, the absorbance at 570 nm was measured with a microplate reader (Molecular Devices SpectraMax M2e). The viability of cells incubated with C-dots was expressed as a percentage of that of untreated cells (set to 100%) according to formula Abs sample/Abs control × 100% (mean ± SD, n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy of intracellular C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Cells (2 × 105 cells) were incubated with C-dots (5 μg/mL) at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h. Cells were then washed twice with PBS, fixed with 3% glutaraldehyde (2 h at room temperature) and embedded in 5% low-melting agarose (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany). Gelatinated blocks were washed in 0.1 M Soerensen's phosphate buffer (Merck, Germany), post-fixed in 1% OsO4 (Roth, Karlsruhe, Germany) in 17% sucrose buffer (Merck, Germany) and dehydrated using an ascending ethanol series (30, 50, 70, 90 and 100%; for 10 min each step). The last step (100% ethanol) was repeated three times. Dehydrated specimens were incubated in propylene oxide (Serva, Germany) for 30 min, in a mixture of Epon resin (Serva, Heidelberg, Germany) and propylene oxide (1:1) for 1 h and finally in pure Epon for 1 h. Epon polymerization was performed at 90 °C for 2 h. Ultrathin sections (70–100 nm) were cut using a Reichert UltracutSultramicrotome (Leica, Germany) equipped with a diamond knife (Diatome, Switzerland) and picked up on copper‑rhodium grids (Plano, Germany). The contrast of the sections was enhanced by standard staining with 0.5% uranyl acetate and 1% lead citrate (both from EMS, Germany). Samples were observed with a transmission electron microscope (Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany).

Fluorescence microscopy was done as described earlier [69,70]. Briefly, cells were seeded on glass cover slips at 5 × 104 cells/well in a 4-well plate. After 24 h of incubation at 37 °C in a humid, 5% CO2 and 95% air atmosphere, cells were incubated with C-dots (100 μg/mL). After 24 h, the culture medium was aspirated, cells were washed 3 times with PBS and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) in PBS for 30 min at room temperature. After fixation, cells were washed twice with PBS and mounted on glass slides using Prolong (Molecular Probes Life technologies). Images were acquired using an Axiovert 200 microscope (Carl Zeiss) equipped with a Plan-Apochromat 100×/1.30 numerical aperture oil immersion objective. Images were recorded with a cooled, back-illuminated charge-coupled device camera (Cascade 512B; Princeton Instruments, USA) driven by IPLab Spectrum software (Scanalytics, USA).

B16F1 and J774.A1 (5 × 104 cells/mL each) cells were seeded in ø 60 Petri dishes. After 24 h, cells were incubated with C-dots (100 μg/mL in DMEM medium) for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Afterward, the medium was aspirated, cells were washed with PBS and then fresh medium was added before starting live cell imaging. For live cell imaging, an Axiovert 200 microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) equipped with a Plan-Apochromat 20× objective and a cooled, back-illuminated charge-coupled device camera (Cascade 512B; Princeton Instruments, Trenton, NJ) driven by IPLab Spectrum software (Scanalytics, Fairfax, USA) was used. During image acquisition, cells were maintained at 37 °C and 5% CO2 using a heating stage and a CO2 controller (PeCon GmbH, Germany). Digital handling of the images was done using ImageJ and Adobe Photoshop (Adobe Systems, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of intracellular C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Flow cytometry analysis of B16F1 melanoma cells and J774.A1 macrophages incubated with C-dots was done using a FACS Canto II from Becton Dickinson. Briefly, cells (1 × 105 cells) were seeded in ø 60 Petri dishes. After 24 h of incubation at 37 °C in a humid, 5% CO2 and 95% air atmosphere, cells were treated with various concentrations of C-dots (50 to 300 μg/mL−1) for different time points (1, 24, 48 and 72 h). Cells without C-dots served as controls. Afterwards, cells were gently removed from Petri dishes, collected and washed with FACS buffer (PBS supplemented with 0.5% FCS and 2 mM EDTA). Approximately 1 × 106 cells were resuspended in 300 μL FACS buffer and the fluorescence intensity was measured in five different channels (Pacific blue, FITC, PE, PE-CY-7 and APC-A). Flow cytometry data were analyzed using FCS Express from DeNovo Software.

Prism 8 (GraphPad Software Inc., USA) was used to generate all graphs. In all bar charts, data are shown as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was done using the one-way ANOVA test in combination with the Tukey method with a statistically significant difference set at p < 0.05.

The simple and one-pot synthesis of highly water-soluble and fluorescent C-dots from Ananas erectifolius fibers is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Briefly, the hydrothermal carbonization of curauá fibers with 1 M sodium hydroxide at 180 °C for 12 h resulted in the generation of C-dots, which, after successive filtration, dialysis and freeze-drying steps were obtained as a brownish yellow solid.

The mechanism of C-dots generation from natural resources is still incompletely understood [20,71,72] and beyond the scope of this study. However, it is worth mentioning that earlier findings showed that the formation of C-dots from such precursors involved two main approaches: i) top-down and ii) bottom-up. In the top-down method, large carbon structures can be converted into small compounds such as carbohydrates and organic acids via the oxidation of water. Over time, the large components are fully digested into individual C-dots. In the bottom-up approach, the intermolecular dehydration and polymerization of carbohydrates facilitates the generation of C-dots [71,72].

Next, we analyzed C-dots at the physico-chemical level. FT-IR analysis showed the characteristic absorption peak at 3338 cm−1 for OH stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (OH) functional groups. The CH stretching vibrations appeared at 2914 cm−1. The CO and CC stretching vibration peaks were visible at 1551 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak for CO stretching vibrations was localized at 1076 cm−1 (Fig. 1A ). In agreement with previous observations [30,31,48,73–76], FT-IR spectroscopy demonstrated the abundant presence of hydroxyl (OH), carbonyl (CO) and carboxylate (OCO) groups as the major chemical functionalities of C-dots. The morphology of C-dots was analyzed by classical TEM (Fig. 1C). C-dots exhibited an almost spherical shape with uniform size dispersion and the absence of signs of aggregation. The analysis of particle size distribution revealed a narrow range from 0.5 to 5 nm with an average diameter of 2.45 nm (Fig. 1B). AFM scans showed the topographic morphology and the height profile of C-dots (Fig. 1D, E and F), which, according to their average diameter, exhibited a height of approximately 2.4 nm (Fig. 1E and F). The surface charge of C-dots was measured to be around −29.1 mV (Fig. S1B).

The chemical composition of C-dots was investigated by XPS analysis. The survey XPS spectrum showed two distinct peaks at 284.0 and 530.6 eV that can be attributed to carbon (C) and oxygen (O) atoms, respectively (Fig. 1G). The high-resolution spectrum of O1s corroborated the presence of two characteristic oxygen states, CO (529.1 eV) and C-OH (530.4 eV) (Fig. 1H). The high-resolution spectrum of the C1s exhibited three major peaks at 281.8, 283.4 and 285.8 eV corresponding to CC, CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 1I). A peak at 281.8 eV confirmed the graphitic structure (sp2, CC) of the C-dots. Moreover, these results revealed that the molecular structure of C-dots mainly consists of carbonyl (CO), carboxylate (OCO) and hydroxyl (OH) groups, in agreement with most of the carbohydrate-derived C-dots reported elsewhere [30,31,48,73]. These functional groups could be attributed to the degradation of the carbohydrates of curauá fibers by the hydrothermal reaction. Notably, the presence of such functional groups at the surface of C-dots certainly contributes to their high solubility and dispersibility in water.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 2A ) of C-dots showed a distinct weak absorption peak at 262 nm that can be attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of CC bonds for nanocarbons, which is consistent with previous findings [21,27]. A strong emission was observed at 452 nm upon the excitation of C-dots with 340 nm light using PL emission spectroscopy (Fig. 2A). We further characterized the PL emission of C-dots at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 520 nm (Figs. 2B and S1A). The C-dots exhibited excellent excitation-dependent PL emission behavior in view of the fact that their emission wavelength shifted from the blue to red with respect to the excitation wavelengths, which could be possibly associated to the different sized NPs and the distribution of different surface emissive traps of the C-dots [48,77].

To test the suitability of C-dots in biological applications, particularly for long-time tracking and imaging, we evaluated the photostability of C-dots under continuous irradiation by a Deuterium lamp for periods up to 60 min using PL spectroscopy. For comparison, we also tested the photostability of a commercial Rhodamine B (RhB) dye, which exhibited an emission profile similar to that exhibited by C-dots. Notably, PL intensity of RhB was almost completely lost after 60 min of irradiation, whereas C-dots lost only 30% of their initial signal (Fig. 2C) clearly indicating that C-dots have a strong resistance to photobleaching thus resulting in a superior photostability compared to commercial RhB dyes [78]. Finally, we addressed the impact of pH on C-dot photophysical properties and found that the PL intensity of C-dots increased with increasing pH reaching the highest values at pH 8 to 12 (Fig. S3A). This observation indicates that the PL emission of C-dots is highly stable under physiological pH (pH 7–8) suggesting that C-dots can be suitable for cellular labeling and imaging.

Previous studies have shown that C-dots can be used for ion sensing, particularly Fe(III) ions [30,44,54–64]. To test whether and which extent C-dots sense metal (Mn+) ions, we incubated C-dots with various Mn+ ions in aqueous solutions and monitored C-dots fluorescence intensity using PL spectroscopy. Among the Mn+ ions tested, Fe(III) ions caused the strongest fluorescence quenching of the C-dots, clearly indicating their selective sensing towards Fe(III) ions (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the high selectivity and sensitivity of C-dots towards Fe(III) ions was confirmed from competitive analyses showing that PL intensity of C-dots was robustly quenched by Fe(III) ions even in the presence of other Mn+ ions at the emission wavelength of 452 nm (Fig. S3B). Such phenomenon could be due to the strong interaction of surface functionalities of C-dots with Mn+ ions [79,80]. The high selectivity of C-dots towards Fe(III) ions could be attributed to the fast electron transfer between the Fe(III) ions and the surface of C-dots containing oxygen-rich groups. In fact, Fe(III) ions display the strongest PL quenching effect against C-dots due to the greater affinity and effective/quick coordination/chelation kinetics between the electron-deficient Fe(III) ions and the electron-rich –COOH and –OH groups on the surface of C-dots, which facilitate the initial electron transition and support the transfer of electrons from C-dots to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe(III) ions resulting in non-radiative electron/hole recombination that leads to fluorescence quenching [21,25] (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots derived from curauá fibers are highly cytocompatible
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies centered on the analysis of the biocompatibility of C-dots were limited to classical cell types such as HeLa [21,30,56,61], MCF-7 [32,56], NIH 3 T3 [22], and A549 [22,60]. Given such limitation, we decided to expand the library of cell types used for the analysis of C-dot biocompatibility by including two new cell types: mouse melanoma B16F1 cells and J774.A1 mouse macrophages. B16F1 cells represent a very well-established cellular model for studying the molecular basis of cell adhesion and motility [85] in tissue engineering, whereas J774.A1 mouse macrophages play a fundamental role in immune surveillance and response to invading pathogens and apoptotic cells [86].

To analyze the potential cytotoxicity of C-dots towards B16F1 and J774.A1 cells, cells were incubated with an amount of C-dots ranging from 50 to 300 μg/mL for 24 h or 48 h. The cytotoxicity of C-dots towards both cell types was determined using an MTT assay. After 24 h, B16F1 and J774.A1 showed a very high viability averaging 96% and 69%, respectively. After 48 h of exposure to C-dots, cell viability averaged 69% and 44% for B16F1 and J774.A1 cells, respectively (Fig. 4A and B ). It is interesting to note that the viability of both cell types was not grossly affected by increasing the concentration of C-dots [87]. In contrast, aminated graphene quantum dots (AG-QDs) reduced the viability of alveolar macrophages (NR8383) in a time and concentration-dependent manner [88]. Besides, it was also demonstrated that nitrogen-phosphorous-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) derived from synthetic precursor induce increasing levels of cellular toxicity in B16F10 melanoma cancer cells as their concentration increases [89]. Our observations indicate that C-dots exhibited excellent cytocompatibility and can be safely used within a wide range of concentrations without grossly affecting cellular viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular internalization and localization of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we aimed at determining whether C-dots are internalized by B16F1 and J774.A1 cells. To this end, we incubated B16F1 and J774.A1 cells with C-dots for 24 h at 37 °C and then analyzed them by TEM. As shown in Fig. 5B and C , C-dots could be detected in the cytoplasm of both cell types (arrows in insets). Interestingly, internalized C-dots are morphological indistinguishable from pure C-dots (Fig. 5A) and can be found in vesicles, which may originate from the endolysosomal compartment (from the endocytotic uptake) or autolysosomes (cell tries to degrade C-dots from cytoplasm in lysosomes). These data clearly indicates that C-dots can be internalized byphagocytic and non-phagocytic cells and could be potentially developed for targeting and labelling specific intracellular compartments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and visualization of C-dots by fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Given the ability of C-dots to emit fluorescent light, we sought to investigate whether they could be used in fluorescence microscopy. To test this possibility, we incubated B16F1 and J774.A1 cells with 100 μg/mL C-dots at 37 °C for 24 h, fixed them with PFA and visualized them using filters sets for Alexa 488 (excitation range of 450–480 nm) and Alexa 594 (excitation range of 510–550 nm). We observed a robust fluorescence signal in both fluorescence channels in both B16F1 (Fig. 6A and B ) and J774.A1 cells (Fig. 6D and E) due to the strong green and red fluorescence emission behavior of C-dots. Moreover, the emission of C-dots detected with the Alexa 488 channel highly overlapped with the C-dots emission detected with the Alexa 594 channels (Fig. 6C and F). Thus, these observations demonstrate that C-dots are suitable for fluorescence microscopy applications in fixed cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curauá-derived C-dots are suitable for long-term live cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The above observations suggest that C-dots could be used for labeling and tracking of cells in live cell imaging applications. To determine their suitability in such applications, B16F1 and J774.A1 cells were incubated with 100 μg/mL of C-dots for 24 h and then monitored by live cell imaging over a period of 24 h. For this analysis, we decided to use only the Alexa 488 channel due to both the stronger emission of C-dots at this wavelength and to the fact that both cell types were sensitive to long exposure times in the excitation range of the Alexa 594 channel. Furthermore, cells were washed with PBS to remove extracellular C-dots, to avoid overlapping of their fluorescence with that of internalized C-dots. As expected, B16F1 and J774.A1 cells emitted a robust fluorescence signal, which remained detectable for several hours (Fig. 7A and B , top panels). This observation is consistent with our observation showing that C-dots have a superior photostability under constant illumination than commercial dyes (see Fig. 2C) and demonstrate that they are suitable for live cell imaging over a period of several hours [90].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of intracellular C-dots

ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we generated novel C-dots from curauá fibers, a green and renewable biomass precursor, via a simple hydrothermal approach. C-dots exhibited excellent physico-chemical features such as high aqueous stability, strong multicolor fluorescence emission and superior photostability. They also exhibited an excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe(III) ions in aqueous environments. In cell biology applications, C-dots exhibited a very good cytocompatibility towards different cell types and could be used as effective probes in fluorescence microscopy, long-term live cell imaging and flow cytometry analysis. Collectively, our findings expand the library of biomass-based C-dots and demonstrate that these novel curauá-derived C-dots can be used as high performance fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe(III) ions, cellular bioimaging and labeling applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank FAPESP (Proc. No. 2015/00094-0; Proc. No. 2017/22017-3), MCTI/SISNANO, REDEAGRONANO for financial support. They also thank Chaolei Hu and Andrij Pich (DWI-Leibniz-Institute for Interactive Materials, RWTH Aachen University) for their assistance in XPS measurement, S. Anbu (Departments of Chemistry and Biomedical Sciences, University of Hull, UK) for his assistance in ion sensing analysis and Shabir Najmudin (Randall Centre, King's College, London, UK) for the revision of English language.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary figures (S1-S6) include photophysical, metal sensing and cellular uptake analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2021.112409.
